title: Inversion after negative and restrictive adverbials
lesson: When a negative or restrictive adverbial is fronted for emphasis, the subject and (auxiliary) verb invert, just as in a question: 'Never have I seen such a mess.' If there is no auxiliary, add a form of 'do': 'Rarely did she complain.' This applies to 'never', 'rarely', 'seldom', 'little', 'hardly/scarcely ... when', 'no sooner ... than', 'not only ... but also', 'only after/when/then', and 'not until'. With 'not only', invert the first clause but keep normal order in the 'but also' clause. With 'hardly ... when' and 'no sooner ... than', the earlier event takes the past perfect.

items.prompt: I have never seen such a beautiful sunset. (begin with 'Never')
answer: Never have I seen such a beautiful sunset.
items.explanation: Fronting the negative adverb 'never' forces subject-auxiliary inversion: the auxiliary 'have' comes before the subject 'I', exactly as in a question.

items.prompt: She had hardly sat down when the phone rang. (begin with 'Hardly')
answer: Hardly had she sat down when the phone rang.
items.explanation: 'Hardly' fronted triggers inversion of the past perfect: 'had she sat down'. The 'hardly ... when' pattern pairs the past perfect (earlier action) with the past simple ('rang').

items.prompt: He had no sooner arrived than he was asked to leave. (begin with 'No sooner')
answer: No sooner had he arrived than he was asked to leave.
items.explanation: 'No sooner' is followed by inverted past perfect ('had he arrived') and is completed by 'than' (not 'when'): 'No sooner had he arrived than ...'

items.prompt: The manager not only apologised but also offered a refund. (begin with 'Not only')
answer: Not only did the manager apologise but also offered a refund.
Not only did the manager apologise but he also offered a refund.
items.explanation: Fronted 'Not only' needs inversion; with no auxiliary present, insert 'did' + bare infinitive: 'did the manager apologise'. The 'but also' clause keeps normal word order.

items.prompt: We rarely realise how lucky we are. (begin with 'Rarely')
answer: Rarely do we realise how lucky we are.
items.explanation: The restrictive adverb 'rarely' fronted requires inversion. As there is no auxiliary, add 'do': 'do we realise'.

items.prompt: I little knew what awaited me. (begin with 'Little')
answer: Little did I know what awaited me.
items.explanation: Fronted 'Little' (in the sense of 'not at all') triggers inversion. With the past simple and no auxiliary, use 'did' + bare infinitive: 'did I know'.

items.prompt: She understood the full danger only after she had left. (begin with 'Only after')
answer: Only after she had left did she understand the full danger.
items.explanation: With 'Only after + clause' fronted, the inversion happens in the MAIN clause, not the time clause: 'did she understand'. The subordinate clause 'she had left' keeps normal order.

items.prompt: He didn't apologise until everyone had gone. (begin with 'Not until')
answer: Not until everyone had gone did he apologise.
items.explanation: 'Not until + clause' fronted causes inversion in the main clause: 'did he apologise'. The negation moves to the front, so the main verb becomes affirmative.

items.prompt: You will succeed only if you work hard. (begin with 'Only if')
answer: Only if you work hard will you succeed.
items.explanation: After a fronted 'Only if + clause', invert the main clause: 'will you succeed'. The conditional clause itself stays in normal order.

items.prompt: I have seldom met a more generous person. (begin with 'Seldom')
answer: Seldom have I met a more generous person.
items.explanation: Fronted 'Seldom' demands inversion of the present perfect auxiliary: 'have I met'.

items.prompt: The truth did not emerge until much later. (begin with 'Not until')
answer: Not until much later did the truth emerge.
items.explanation: 'Not until + time phrase' fronted triggers inversion with 'did': 'did the truth emerge'. The negation is carried by 'Not until', so the verb is affirmative.

items.prompt: At no point did she lose her composure. (rewrite without inversion, beginning 'She')
answer: She did not lose her composure at any point.
She didn't lose her composure at any point.
items.explanation: Reversing the process: the fronted negative 'At no point' caused inversion. Returning to normal order, the negation reattaches to the verb ('did not lose') and 'no point' becomes 'any point'.

items.prompt: We understood the gravity of the situation only then. (begin with 'Only then')
answer: Only then did we understand the gravity of the situation.
items.explanation: A fronted 'Only then' is a restrictive adverbial that triggers inversion in the main clause. With the past simple and no auxiliary, insert 'did' + bare infinitive: 'did we understand'.

items.prompt: You must not open this door on any account. (begin with 'On no account')
answer: On no account must you open this door.
items.explanation: The fronted negative adverbial 'On no account' forces subject-auxiliary inversion: the modal 'must' moves before the subject 'you'. The negation is carried by the adverbial, so the verb itself is affirmative.

items.prompt: The performance was so impressive that the audience rose to their feet. (begin with 'So')
answer: So impressive was the performance that the audience rose to their feet.
items.explanation: Fronting 'So + adjective' for emphasis triggers inversion of the verb 'be': 'was the performance'. The 'that' result clause that follows keeps normal word order.

items.prompt: Visitors are not permitted to enter the laboratory under any circumstances. (begin with 'Under no circumstances')
answer: Under no circumstances are visitors permitted to enter the laboratory.
items.explanation: The fronted negative phrase 'Under no circumstances' demands inversion: the auxiliary 'are' precedes the subject 'visitors'. The negation sits in the adverbial, leaving the verb affirmative.

items.prompt: You can solve this problem only by working together. (begin with 'Only by')
answer: Only by working together can you solve this problem.
items.explanation: A fronted 'Only by + -ing' is a restrictive adverbial that triggers inversion in the main clause: the modal 'can' moves before the subject 'you'. The gerund phrase 'working together' itself keeps normal order.

items.prompt: The damage was so great that the bridge had to be rebuilt. (begin with 'Such')
answer: Such was the damage that the bridge had to be rebuilt.
items.explanation: Fronting 'Such' for emphasis triggers inversion of the verb 'be': 'was the damage'. The 'that' result clause that follows keeps normal word order. ('Such + be' parallels the 'So + adjective + be' pattern.)

items.prompt: If I had known about the delay, I would have rebooked the flight. (omit 'if', begin with 'Had')
answer: Had I known about the delay, I would have rebooked the flight.
items.explanation: In a third conditional, dropping 'if' inverts the auxiliary 'had' to the front: 'Had I known ...'. The main clause ('I would have rebooked the flight') is unchanged.

items.prompt: If the bridge were to collapse, the whole town would be cut off. (omit 'if', begin with 'Were')
answer: Were the bridge to collapse, the whole town would be cut off.
items.explanation: With 'were to' in a hypothetical conditional, omitting 'if' fronts 'were' before the subject: 'Were the bridge to collapse ...'. This formal inversion replaces 'if' entirely.

items.prompt: If you should need any assistance, please do not hesitate to ask. (omit 'if', begin with 'Should')
answer: Should you need any assistance, please do not hesitate to ask.
items.explanation: In a conditional with 'should' (a tentative future possibility), dropping 'if' moves 'should' to the front before the subject: 'Should you need ...'. The bare infinitive 'need' follows.

items.prompt: She has not enjoyed a film this much since she was a child. (begin with 'Not since')
answer: Not since she was a child has she enjoyed a film this much.
items.explanation: The fronted negative time phrase 'Not since + clause' triggers inversion in the MAIN clause: the auxiliary 'has' precedes the subject 'she'. The subordinate clause 'she was a child' keeps normal order.

items.prompt: The manager not only apologised but also offered a refund. (begin with 'Not only')
answer: Not only did the manager apologise but he also offered a refund.
Not only did the manager apologise but also offered a refund.
items.explanation: Fronted 'Not only' needs inversion; with no auxiliary present, insert 'did' + bare infinitive: 'did the manager apologise'. The 'but also' clause keeps normal word order.

items.prompt: This decision does not reflect our official policy in any way. (begin with 'In no way')
answer: In no way does this decision reflect our official policy.
items.explanation: The fronted negative adverbial 'In no way' forces subject-auxiliary inversion. With no auxiliary in the original, insert 'does' + bare infinitive: 'does this decision reflect'. The negation is carried by the adverbial, so the verb itself is affirmative.

items.prompt: You will see such abundant wildlife nowhere else in Europe. (begin with 'Nowhere else')
answer: Nowhere else in Europe will you see such abundant wildlife.
items.explanation: The fronted negative place adverbial 'Nowhere else' triggers subject-auxiliary inversion: the modal 'will' precedes the subject 'you'. The negation sits in 'Nowhere', leaving the verb affirmative.

items.prompt: She said not a word throughout the entire meeting. (begin with 'Not a word')
answer: Not a word did she say throughout the entire meeting.
items.explanation: The fronted negative object 'Not a word' triggers inversion. With the past simple and no auxiliary, insert 'did' + bare infinitive: 'did she say'. The negation is carried by the fronted object, so the verb stays affirmative.
